title: Isaiah 46
subtitle: Babylon’s Gods Carried vs God Who Carries

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “I Have Made You and I Will Carry You”

heb.paragraph: "Even to your old age and grey hairs I am he, I am he who will sustain you. I have made you and I will carry (sābal) you; I will sustain you and I will rescue you" (v.4). The contrast: Bel and Nebo (Babylon’s chief gods) are loaded onto weary animals — the gods must be CARRIED (vv.1–2). But Israel’s God CARRIES his people: from birth to old age, from creation to grey hairs (vv.3–4). Pagan gods are burdens; YHWH bears burdens. The idol-maker pays gold, hires a craftsman, then carries the idol and sets it in its place — "it cannot move; it cannot save" (v.7).

notes.note: "Bel bows down, Nebo stoops low; their idols are borne by beasts of burden." Babylon’s New Year procession: gods paraded on carts. But the carts are fleeing — the gods cannot save even themselves.
"Even to your old age I am he. I have made you and I will carry you." The lifetime promise: womb to grave. God’s carrying never expires.
"They carry it on their shoulders; they set it in its place, and there it stands. It cannot save." The idol-test: can it move? Can it answer? Can it save? No, no, no.

sections.header: Verses 8–13 — “I Am God, and There Is No Other”

heb.paragraph: "I make known the end from the beginning, from ancient times, what is still to come. I say, ‘My purpose (ʿēṣâ) will stand, and I will do all that I please’" (v.10). "Remember this, you rebels. I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like me" (v.9). God summons "a bird of prey from the east, a man to fulfil my purpose from a far-off land" (v.11) — Cyrus again. "What I have said, that I will bring about; what I have planned, that I will do" (v.11). "My righteousness is not far away; my salvation will not be delayed" (v.13).

notes.note: "I am God and there is none like me. I make known the end from the beginning." The test of deity: can you declare the future? Only YHWH passes this test. The idol-trial theme culminates here.
"A bird of prey from the east. What I have planned, that I will do. My salvation will not be delayed." Cyrus as raptor: swift, unstoppable, divinely directed. God’s timetable is not behind schedule.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVEven to your old age and gray hairs I am he, I am he who will sustain you. I have made you and I will carry you; I will sustain you and I will rescue you.ESVeven to your old age I am he, and to gray hairs I will carry you. I have made, and I will bear; I will carry and will save.NIVThey lift it to their shoulders and carry it; they set it up in its place, and there it stands. From that spot it cannot move. Even though someone cries out to it, it cannot answer; it cannot save them from their troubles.ESVThey lift it to their shoulders, they carry it, they set it in its place, and it stands there; it cannot move from its place. If one cries to it, it does not answer or save him from his trouble.

rec.text: ""Bel bows down, Nebo stoops low; their idols are borne by beasts of burden." Babylon’s New Year procession: gods paraded on carts. But the carts are fleeing — the gods cannot save even themselves."

rows.label: The End from the Beginning — 46:8–13
rows.text: "I make known the END from the BEGINNING." "What I have planned, I WILL DO." Cyrus the bird of prey.

hebtext.note: "Even to your old age and grey hairs I am he, I am he who will sustain you. I have made you and I will carry (sābal) you; I will sustain you and I will rescue you" (v.4). The contrast: Bel and Nebo (Babylon’s chief gods) are loaded onto weary animals — the gods must be CARRIED (vv.1–2). But Israel’s God CARRIES his people: from birth to old age, from creation to grey hairs (vv.3–4). Pagan gods are burdens; YHWH bears burdens. The idol-maker pays gold, hires a craftsman, then carries the idol and sets it in its place — "it cannot move; it cannot save" (v.7).

"I make known the end from the beginning, from ancient times, what is still to come. I say, ‘My purpose (ʿēṣâ) will stand, and I will do all that I please’" (v.10). "Remember this, you rebels. I am God, and there is no other; I am God, and there is none like me" (v.9). God summons "a bird of prey from the east, a man to fulfil my purpose from a far-off land" (v.11) — Cyrus again. "What I have said, that I will bring about; what I have planned, that I will do" (v.11). "My righteousness is not far away; my salvation will not be delayed" (v.13).

themes.note: Sovereignty peaks at 10: God carries; idols are carried. "I make known the end from the beginning." What I plan, I do.
